headline: FIFA Schedules World Cup Into Unsafe Heat
deck: FIFPRO recommends cooling breaks at 26°C wet-bulb globe temperature and postponement at 28°C. FIFA weighs stopping play only above 32°C. About a quarter of this summer's 104 matches are projected inside the lower band, while cities declare Heat Health Emergencies and FIFA mandates hydration stops in every game.

art.caption: The World Cup is played in heat its own players' union calls unsafe; the schedule serves the broadcast window

body: FIFPRO's heat-stress guidance recommends cooling breaks once wet-bulb globe temperature reaches 26°C and treats 28°C as the point where postponement should be considered because conditions are unsafe for play [E1]. FIFA's own regulations for the tournament set a higher bar, weighing match postponement only when WBGT climbs above 32°C [E2]. The six-degree spread between when the players' union says cooling must begin and when the federation will even consider stopping play reflects a policy choice about how much heat exposure competition will absorb before the schedule yields [E1][E2].

A World Weather Attribution analysis of the 2026 fixture list projects that 26 of the 104 World Cup matches will kick off at or above 26°C WBGT, roughly a quarter of the tournament [E2]. About five fixtures are expected to reach or exceed 28°C, the threshold FIFPRO marks as unsafe [E1][E2]. Those fixtures land inside a summer calendar FIFA fixed for global broadcast audiences [E2]. The union's thresholds and the projected match-day conditions overlap often enough that the guidance was written for this exact tournament profile [E1][E2].

For 2026, FIFA imposed mandatory hydration breaks in every match regardless of the thermometer reading, framing the rule as a player-welfare measure [E3]. Universal breaks concede that heat risk is baked into the schedule even on cooler days, because temperature-triggered pauses would admit that many kickoffs should never have been set for mid-afternoon sun [E3]. The federation did not lower the postponement threshold to match FIFPRO's 28°C line [E1][E2]. It distributed water stops across the full slate and kept play running [E3].

Philadelphia declared a Heat Health Emergency extended through 5 July as heat-index values pushed toward 110°F, and the city rolled out match-day cooling measures for the World Cup knockout played there on 4 July [E4]. On opening day in Houston, more than 100 heat-related illnesses were reported at the fan festival surrounding the tournament, most treated on site [E5]. Municipal governments moved faster than FIFA's rulebook as crowds outside the stadium strained under the same summer conditions the fixture list assigned [E4][E5]. Heat Health Emergencies and festival triage lines formed a second layer of protection the federation's universal hydration breaks never replaced [E3][E4][E5].

FIFA's Training Centre publishes per-match physical telemetry in public post-match reports, including sprint and top-speed readings distributed as technical PDFs [E6]. Those files supply performance data, but the signal is too context-heavy to isolate a clean heat effect without a controlled comparison sample [E6]. Scheduling officials weighed moving the Mexico-England round-of-16 kickoff at the Azteca over severe-weather risk, then held the 6 p.m. local start [E7]. The elements were on the agenda; the slot stayed fixed for the window that mattered [E7].

From these pieces, summer kickoff slots and a 32°C postponement bar function as a broadcast-and-legacy compromise dressed in neutral safety language [E2][E3][E7]. Hydration breaks in every match, city Heat Health Emergencies, and a quarter of fixtures projected inside FIFPRO's cooling-break zone describe a tournament designed for July audiences while athletes compete at temperatures their own representatives call unsafe [E1][E2][E3][E4]. Public performance PDFs cannot prove harm cleanly from the published record alone, and that ambiguity protects the schedule as much as the data gap frustrates critics [E6]. When proof requires a controlled trial the federation never authorized, absence of evidence becomes convenient cover for a broadcast-first calendar [E6].

Summer is when the international football calendar actually clears, and the heat bearing down on Houston, Philadelphia, and Mexico City is weather as much as fixture-setting [E2]. Mandatory hydration breaks in all matches and municipal cooling deployments are real protections that cities and FIFA put in place before kickoff [E3][E4]. No player collapse has been documented at this tournament, and the physical reports that exist were never built to settle a medical verdict in real time [E6]. Holding that record at full strength does not erase the threshold gap between FIFPRO and FIFA; it marks what the public evidence can and cannot yet show [E1][E2][E6]. Officials who kept the Mexico-England kickoff at 6 p.m. faced genuine severe-weather risk and still judged the slot workable [E7].

Fans packed Houston's opening festival until medics logged more than a hundred heat-related illnesses in a single afternoon [E5]. Philadelphia called a Heat Health Emergency while the knockout went ahead under the same summer sun the broadcast window demanded [E4]. The colosseum fills on schedule; the body absorbs what the clock refused to postpone.

key_numbers.label: Houston fan-festival heat cases, opening day
